Name: Scotland Yard Pub
Brand Positioning: A relaxed, English-style pub where locals and visitors can unwind with drink specials and classic pub fare throughout the week.
Concept Identity: Scotland Yard Pub is a classic English-style neighbourhood pub in downtown Toronto, proud of its long heritage. It sits comfortably at the intersection of comfort, sport, and casual camaraderie. Its atmosphere is warm, approachable, and familiar. A place you go to unwind, watch the game, share hearty food, drink a pint, and feel part of the crowd. The décor and service underscore tradition and friendliness rather than luxury or pretension. It leans heavily into its sports culture (especially soccer) with many screens, big events, and a sense of community (fans, regulars). The visual and experiential cues likely include dark woods, cozy lighting, lots of wood/barrel/patio spaces, mixed seating (bar stools, tables, couches), and signage that’s not overly stylised. The tone of voice is welcoming, unpretentious, warm, maybe with some playful nods to British pub traditions (menu names, menu layout, etc.). Price points are reasonable, not “fine dining,” but offering good value. It also supports group bookings, private events, and varied day-parts (from brunch/late breakfast to late night).
Target Audience:
25-55
Men
Single
Heavy Drinker
Sports Fans
 
1km Distance
 
Value Driven
Social
Atmosphere & Experience: Comfortable, casual, classic English pub vibe. Many TVs/big screens for watching soccer and other sports. Outdoor patio. Welcoming to both groups and solo visitors. Friendly service. Lots of drink specials and daily/weekly promotions.
Menu Features:
British comfort food + Canadian classics
Draft beer selection (local + international) 
Handcrafted cocktails
Menu items include “British-style pub fare".
Common google review themes:
Friendly, long-standing staff and consistent service
 
Great atmosphere for watching sports
 
Solid pub food and draft beer selection
 
Busy during games; seating can be limited
USP:
Consistency
Longer hourse, atmosphere
 
Value
Cheaper drinks
 
Communty
Recognition by name, Familiarity
Merch / Extensions: Limited branded apparel (t-shirts, hats) occasionally available; focus on community events and sports partnerships rather than merchandise. No active e-commerce presence.
Event Programming / Regular Events: Sports screenings (Premier League, NHL, NFL). Occasional special events for holidays i.e St Patricks Day and major games. Active during major tournaments; strong sports-community turnout.
Accessibility Features: Wheelchair-accessible entrance and washrooms. Ground-level layout with wide aisles and clear bar visibility.
Delivery / Takeout / Online Ordering: Available through Uber Eats and DoorDash. Takeout orders accepted by phone or in person. Online reservation system via website.
Promotions & Specials Schedule: Seasonal specials, Daily "Yard sale" between 2pm-5pm & 7pm - 10pm. Bottomless brunch on weekends - $20 Mimosas and Caesars
Collaborations / Partnerships: Unofficial collabs with Tottenham \ Spurs fan club
Community / Local Engagement: Long-standing St. Lawrence Market community fixture. Hosts local sports team gatherings, charity fundraisers, and holiday drives. Loyal regular base fosters a neighborhood “local’s pub” culture.
Email / Newsletter Presence: Website contact form for inquiries; no active newsletter or email marketing program
Capacity (Seated / Standing): Approx. 120 seated / 160 standing across main dining and bar areas.
Peak Hours / Visit Times: Evenings (6–10 PM) and weekends during sports games. Lunchtime crowd steady on weekdays.
Visual Style / Branding Aesthetic: Classic British pub design. Dark wood, warm lighting, brass accents, and vintage decor. Branding emphasizes authenticity, heritage, and comfort.

Name: Score on King
Location: 107 King St E, Toronto, ON M5C 1G6
Brand Positioning: A lively, sports-focused pub known for its creative Caesar cocktails, welcoming patio, and approachable menu making it a go-to spot for fans looking for a fun, casual game-day experience.
Direct or Indirect Competitor: Direct: Similar casual, energetic pub environment with strong food and drink focus; attracts a young, social downtown crowd. Competes for the same after-work and weekend traffic.
Concept Identity: Score on King is a modern, high-energy sports pub focused on being a social destination for younger crowds and sports fans. It balances the excitement of live games with broad appeal through late-night dining, brunch, drinks, and a vibrant patio culture. Its visual and experiential cues lean toward bold, dynamic, and somewhat edgy, not understated or formal. The interior emphasizes screens, communal seating, loud ambient energy, open spaces, bright/colourful lighting, and a lively staff presence. Messaging is direct, confident, and community-oriented (“Score Army”), aiming to make patrons feel like part of a fun, spirited group. Food & drink offerings span day to late night, giving it flexibility and consistent traffic across many hours.
Target Audience: Younger crowd, sports fans, groups of friends, people who enjoy late night and socializing. Also patrons who enjoy patios and street life, since there is a street-side patio.
Atmosphere & Experience: Sports pub, lively vibe. Street-side patio on Church St (or near Church St); lots of seating optimized for watching games. The tone is energetic, youthful, somewhat irreverent
Menu Features: Modern pub food with a focus on indulgence and shareability. Famous “Caesar towers,” stacked burgers, wings, nachos, and brunch plates. Wide beer selection, signature cocktails, and bold presentation for social media appeal.
Website Quality: Strong: Their website is modern and user-friendly, reflecting their vibrant atmosphere. It offers clear information on their menu, events, and location. The site aligns well with their energetic brand identity.
Common google review themes:
Fun, energetic vibe. Ideal for groups and sports nights
 
Huge, creative portions (especially Caesars)
 
Lively staff and social-media-worthy presentations
 
Occasional long wait times or loud environment noted
USP: High-energy sports bar famous for its outrageous food presentations (notably the “Caesar towers”) and shareable, social dining experience
Guarantee: High-energy, crowd-pleasing fun with bold food and memorable presentations
Merch / Extensions: Branded merchandise (shirts, hats, and Caesar-themed gear) tied to social promotions. Strong digital extension through social media content and influencer collaborations.
Event Programming / Regular Events: Game-day specials, themed sports nights, and seasonal brunch events. Occasional promotional contests and social media-driven events tied to major sports moments.
Private / Group Booking Capabilities: Online reservation system for large parties (7+). Spacious layout and patio suitable for group celebrations, birthdays, and watch parties. No fully private room but highly group-friendly.
Accessibility Features: Street-level entrance and washrooms reported accessible. Open-concept layout; staff noted as accommodating.
Delivery / Takeout / Online Ordering: Active on DoorDash, SkipTheDishes, and Uber Eats. Takeout available for pickup. Online booking and order links integrated through website and social channels.
Promotions & Specials Schedule: Weekday Happy Hour 2–5 PM. Game-day drink specials, brunch features on weekends, and social media-exclusive contests.
Collaborations / Partnerships: Works with local alcohol brands and influencers for product promotions. Partners with sports organizations during major tournaments. Strong social media collaborations.
Community / Local Engagement: Engages heavily through social media and influencer networks. Sponsors local events and sports-viewing nights. Builds community around social experiences rather than grassroots programs.
Email / Newsletter Presence: Active website contact and inquiry form; Online newsletter sign up. Uses social media as main communication channel for updates and promotions.
Capacity (Seated / Standing): Approx. 150 seated / 200 standing including patio and indoor sections.
Peak Hours / Visit Times: Evenings (5–10 PM), especially on game nights and weekends. Strong brunch traffic on Saturdays and Sundays.
Visual Style / Branding Aesthetic: Bold, modern, and high-energy. Bright colors, neon signage, and large TV screens dominate the space. Social-media-friendly visuals and youthful, vibrant tone.

Name: Duke's Refresher St Lawrence
Location: 73 Front St E, Toronto, ON M5E 1V9
Brand Positioning: A laid-back social pub where guests can unwind over drinks and classic games like table tennis, arcade favorites, and mini basketball, with a patio that extends the fun outdoors.
Direct or Indirect Competitor: Direct: Also targets the same audience, downtown professionals and groups, with approachable food, beers, and cocktails in a casual, lively space.
Concept Identity: Duke’s Refresher (St. Lawrence Market) is a nostalgic, energetic bar & grill hybrid that blends food, drink, and play in a casual, retro-vibe setting. It’s a place where people come to let loose to enjoy craft beer, share hearty food (burgers, pizza, fries, etc.), and engage in fun distractions (arcade games, ping-pong, mini basketball). The décor, programming, and offerings lean into 1970s nostalgia and vintage style, using playful art (velvet paintings), bold drink and beer selection, and eclectic ambiance. The tone is unpretentious and friendly: you don’t have to dress up or guard your behaviour, you come for entertainment, socializing, and relaxing. Its target is those who want more than just dinner; they want an experience that includes food + drinks + atmosphere + fun. It positions itself as a destination for nights and weekends, with brunch options that extend the experience into daytime.
Target Audience: People looking for a fun, social nightlife/dining experience; younger adults; groups of friends; people who enjoy food + drink + entertainment together. Also weekend brunchers.
Atmosphere & Experience:
Strong nostalgic / retro feel: arcade games (Taxi Pinball, Galaxian, etc.), mini basketball court, ping pong, pool, shuffleboard. 
 
Decor inspired by the 1970s; vintage décor, retro inspired rooms. 
 
There’s also a gallery of velvet paintings, added artistic / quirky twist.
Menu Features: American-style pub menu featuring burgers, sandwiches, tacos, and loaded appetizers. 40+ beers on tap. Emphasis on casual dining, fun comfort food, and large portions. Late-night offerings and themed drink specials.
Website Quality: Strong: Their website is straightforward, offering essential information like hours and location. While functional, it could benefit from more engaging content to match the lively atmosphere of the venue.
Common google review themes:
Casual, upbeat environment with attentive service
 
Affordable daily specials and happy hour deals
 
Popular for late-night drinks and games
 
Mixed feedback on food consistency and noise level
USP: Playful, casual venue blending bar games, affordable drinks, and lively service. Appeals to the social, spontaneous crowd looking for a fun night rather than a formal dinner.
Guarantee: Casual, carefree good times with affordable drinks, approachable food, and upbeat service
Merch / Extensions: Occasional branded apparel and glassware; extends brand through drink specials, arcade nights, and social campaigns. Active engagement with nightlife events and partnerships.
Event Programming / Regular Events: Trivia Tuesdays, live DJ sets, and late-night social activations. Frequent happy hour specials and themed nights (e.g., “Wing Wednesdays”).
Private / Group Booking Capabilities: Group bookings available via website or phone. Can accommodate 15–30 guests with semi-private seating areas. Ideal for casual parties and corporate socials.
Accessibility Features: Accessible entrance and washrooms. Bar and seating areas have varied heights; some sections more limited for mobility devices.
Delivery / Takeout / Online Ordering: Delivery via Uber Eats. Offers takeout and pickup options. Promotes digital ordering through website during peak seasons.
Promotions & Specials Schedule: Daily specials and late-night Happy Hour Sunday–Thursday (4–6 PM, 10 PM–Close). Weekly themed events like “Wing Wednesdays.”
Collaborations / Partnerships: Regular partnerships with beer and spirits brands for tap takeovers and seasonal activations. Participates in local bar crawls and nightlife promotions.
Community / Local Engagement: Active in nightlife and bar crawls around downtown Toronto. Participates in charitable promotions and social giveaways. Appeals strongly to young, urban locals.
Email / Newsletter Presence: No visible newsletter program. Uses social channels and Google listings for updates and promotions.
Capacity (Seated / Standing): Approx. 130 seated / 180 standing. Flexible layout for large groups and events.
Peak Hours / Visit Times: Late evenings (8 PM–1 AM) and weekends. Strong after-work crowd Thursday–Friday.
Visual Style / Branding Aesthetic: Retro-industrial with exposed brick, playful signage, and neon elements. Laid-back, humorous brand personality focused on nightlife and fun.

Name: The Flatiron: A Firkin Pub
Location: 49 Wellington St E, Toronto, ON M5E 1C9
Brand Positioning: A classic British-style pub set in a historic building, offering an inviting patio and a global selection of beers and hearty pub fare for those seeking tradition with a touch of international flavor.
Direct or Indirect Competitor: Direct: Another long-standing British-style pub with comparable menu, vibe, and clientele; shares overlap in both concept and audience.
Concept Identity: The Flatiron: A Firkin Pub is a heritage-style British pub located in Toronto’s historic Gooderham Building (Flatiron). It blends traditional British charm with a cozy, social atmosphere, serving classic pub fare, pints, and cocktails in a setting that attracts both downtown professionals and tourists. The concept emphasizes heritage, familiarity, and community, reflecting the Firkin Group’s signature brand of approachable British pub culture.
Target Audience:
Ages 25–50
 
Local professionals, tourists, and after-work patrons
 
Enjoy traditional pub environments with historic ambiance
 
Seeking reliability, classic food, and approachable service
Atmosphere & Experience: Historic and inviting. Set in Toronto’s iconic Flatiron building, it carries a strong sense of heritage. Wood furnishings, traditional décor, and a mix of locals and tourists create a warm, comfortable environment. Balanced between cozy pub charm and lively energy, especially on weekends.
Menu Features: Classic British pub menu. Shepherd’s pie, bangers & mash, wings, and fish & chips. Complemented by Firkin Group’s standard offerings of burgers, wraps, and share plates. Solid beer and cocktail list; reliable and consistent options.
Website Quality: Strong: Their website is user friendly, slightly busy but interactive and includes their blog for additional content. It offers clear information on their menu, events, and locations
Common google review themes:
Iconic building and tourist appeal
 
Friendly, accommodating staff
 
Reliable food quality and good beer selection
 
Occasional crowding and slower service during peak hours
USP: Historic landmark location under the Gooderham Building; offers classic British pub comfort with consistent Firkin-brand hospitality. Reliable, familiar, and iconic in its setting.
Guarantee: Consistent Firkin-brand hospitality and reliable British-pub favorites delivered in an iconic, historic setting guests can trust.
Merch / Extensions: No dedicated merch; brand extensions occur through the broader Firkin Pub Group network (gift cards, loyalty promotions, Firkin-wide events).
Event Programming / Regular Events: Sports viewings, Firkin Group events, and select holiday promotions. Occasional live music or trivia depending on the season.
Private / Group Booking Capabilities: Private upper-level space available for events. Works with the Firkin Group’s event booking system for larger gatherings. Common for tourist groups and corporate lunches.
Accessibility Features: Main floor partially accessible; upper level accessible only by stairs due to heritage building limitations.
Delivery / Takeout / Online Ordering: Delivery and pickup through Uber Eats and SkipTheDishes. Orders also accepted via phone. Firkin Group website provides digital menu and ordering links.
Loyalty / Rewards Program: Part of the Firkin Pub Group network; guests can earn rewards and receive offers through the group’s promotional mailing list
Promotions & Specials Schedule: Firkin Group’s rotating daily specials, typically discounted pints and appetizers. Seasonal promotions and sports-night offers.
Collaborations / Partnerships: Operates within the Firkin Pub Group, sharing network partnerships with national suppliers and breweries. Occasionally collaborates on city-wide Firkin events.
Community / Local Engagement: Participates in St. Lawrence neighborhood events and seasonal Firkin Group campaigns. Attracts both locals and tourists; less formal community programming but steady repeat patronage.
Email / Newsletter Presence: Part of the Firkin Group network, which sends group-wide promotional emails and offers. No location-specific newsletter. They have an active Blog
Capacity (Seated / Standing): Approx. 82 seated / 110 patio (combined capacity ~190 total). Multi-level space allows adaptable seating.
Peak Hours / Visit Times: After-work hours (5–9 PM) and weekends. Patio busiest in summer months; tourist foot traffic steady year-round.
Visual Style / Branding Aesthetic: Historic British charm, vintage pub aesthetic with dark tones and cozy interiors. Consistent with Firkin Group branding: traditional, approachable, and familiar.

Name: The Loose Moose
Location: 146 Front St W, Toronto, ON M5J 1G2
Brand Positioning: A festive, high-energy downtown bar/restaurant landmark offering craft-beer variety, solid food (burgers etc.), and a social-hub experience aimed at locals and visitors alike. They lean into their heritage (since 1989), their “beer & burger” cred, and their proximity to major entertainment/sports venues. Big competitor as a sports bar.
Direct or Indirect Competitor:
Direct: Both are downtown Toronto bar/restaurants targeting the same social, after-work, and pre/post-event crowd. SY & The Loose Moose compete for location traffic, occasions (pre- and post-game drinks, group gatherings, casual dining), Audience (young professionals, locals, and visitors looking for a lively pub/bar experience). 

Beer-heavy menus, comfort food (burgers/pub fare), and a social, energetic atmosphere.

The go-to downtown spot for drinks, food, and sports-night socializing, differing mainly in vibe (Loose Moose = large, high-energy beer bar; Scotland Yard = cozier, traditional pub).
Concept Identity: A legendary downtown beer hall for letting loose. It’s built around heritage, scale, and sociability. A high energy, unpretentious gathering place that blends the spirit of a classic pub with the buzz of a modern beer hall.
Target Audience:
Young professionals, city dwellers, and groups of friends working or living in downtown Toronto.

Enjoy casual nights out with food, drinks, and energy. Not fine dining, but still quality-driven.

Value a fun, social, and lively environment that feels iconic and familiar. Big Sports fans.



“Work hard, play hard” crowd looking to unwind after work or before/after an event (Typically sporting events).

Choose venues with vibe, convenience, and credibility (a place everyone knows).

Appreciate large beer selections, hearty food, and friendly service.
Atmosphere & Experience: Busy, social, and full of personality. The space has a warm, vintage meets industrial look with wood, brick, and neon touches that give it a casual but character-filled feel. It’s the kind of place where you can come for one beer and end up staying for three hours.
Menu Features:
Epic Burgers (Their Core Draw)

Known for their house-made, over-the-top burgers with creative toppings, quality patties, and house-baked buns.

Menu highlights have included items like:
The un-origional Burger line: Classic burgers but elevated.
The “Epic” Burger line: premium toppings like smoked bacon, onion rings, or specialty cheeses.

They also serve "Shareables" like Nachos, sandwiches and even Fish & Chips.
Website Quality:
Strong: It aligns well with the brand identity (bold, social, beer & burger venue), provides the necessary information, and is user-friendly. It represents a strong foundation. The brand tone is clear, there is good content to leverage, and it is credible.

From a competitive/strategic perspective though, there’s room to elevate the website to become a stronger marketing asset: more interactive content, better mobile optimization (particularly for menus/deals), stronger integration with social/community management, and enhanced conversion mechanisms (reservations, app download, loyalty).

Positive themes:
Vast beer & drink selection

Solid food, especially burgers & pub fare

Good for pre/post game or group outings

Friendly and attentive service

Energetic, fun atmosphere


Negative themes:
Noise & crowd-levels. Because it’s popular and high-energy, some say it gets loud and busy, which can impact conversation. 

Waits and seating during peak times

Alcohol/spirit pricing concerns. A few reviewers point out drinks can be expensive, especially speciality shots.
USP: It combines a standout beer & burger experience with a high-energy and central-city social setting.
Guarantee: Implied Guarantees: They promise inventive plates, generous portions and the cleanest taps in the city.

There are vintage/second-hand T-shirts from the Loose Moose (bar/restaurant) available on Etsy (e.g., a 1990s “Loose Moose Tap & Grill Toronto” tee).

Nothing available on their website.
Event Programming / Regular Events:
Live‑music nights in the basement venue, The Antler Room, featuring local bands playing everything from rock and indie to Top 40 and alternative. 

Drink specials (e.g., pints, beers on promotion) tied to weekday or game‑night occasions. 

Pre‑game / post‑game social gatherings (given the location near major sports/entertainment venues) which act like regular “event” occasions even if not formally scheduled. 

Themed music or nostalgia nights (e.g., “Big Shiny 90s” event) in the Antler Room.
Private / Group Booking Capabilities:
The main floor can accommodate up to 300 guests for private events. 

Their downstairs space, the The Antler Room, can hold up to 100 seated / 400 standing (reception style) for functions. 

Full buy‐out possibilities: when main space + Antler Room combined, capacity is reported “as many as 800 guests” in total. 

The venue offers dedicated AV system, wireless internet, and event amenities to support presentations, live music, and receptions.

“Communal Section”, “Front Cocktail”, etc similar subdivisions mentioned in event menu.
Accessibility Features: Partially accessible. They have features like wheelchair access to certain spaces, but there may be limitations when it comes to washrooms, elevator access, and ground‐level entry.
Delivery / Takeout / Online Ordering:
They provide take‑out and delivery from the restaurant, via their website and through third‑party platforms. 

Their official site advertises free delivery when ordering via their website or their “Inspired Rewards” app (for certain items / conditions). 

They are listed on platforms such as Uber Eats. 

They have a dedicated online ordering portal for take‑out/delivery: “order.theloosemoose.ca”.
Loyalty / Rewards Program:
Yes, The Loose Moose participates in a loyalty and rewards program via its parent company, SIR Corp.

The program is called the SIR Loyalty Program (also known as “Inspired Rewards”). 

It is free to join; no purchase necessary to enroll. 

Members can earn points when dining at participating SIR restaurants (including The Loose Moose) and redeem rewards, access events, or unlock special perks. 

The Loose Moose also advertises use of the “Inspired Rewards App” for ordering and mentions “free delivery” when ordering via that app. 

Additionally, The Loose Moose is a partner in the “Key To The City” VIP program (which gives a complimentary appetizer to keyholders dining in) though this is a separate.
Promotions & Specials Schedule:
Free Delivery” when you order favourites via their website or the Inspired Rewards app. 

“Light & Dark Duo” beer special: 2 beers for $7. 

VIP perk via the Key To The City program: For Keyholders dining in, a complimentary appetizer (up to $18 value) with minimum spend. 

“Beer of the Month” features/taps highlighted on their social channels (e.g., “Oktoberfest Bier” in their recent posts) and on their menu.
Collaborations / Partnerships: Limited formal collaborations. The Loose Moose is part of the SIR Corp. “Signature” restaurant group, which means the brand is aligned with the broader corporate portfolio and may share cross‑brand promotional/marketing ties but unable to find.
Community / Local Engagement:
The Loose Moose has been a long‑standing fixture in the downtown Toronto scene (since 1989) and is described as a “social hub” for locals and visitors alike. 

They host live music in their basement venue (the “The Antler Room”) featuring local bands, which supports the local music community. 

The venue plays into the pre‑game / post‑game crowd (sports and entertainment nearby) which means it magnetises groups and visitors, thereby engaging with local social/entertainment culture.
Email / Newsletter Presence: Email sign up available on website but no idication of what the content is for a newsletter or email blasts.
Capacity (Seated / Standing):
The main floor can accommodate up to 300 guests for private events. 

Their downstairs space, the The Antler Room, can hold up to 100 seated / 400 standing (reception style) for functions. 

Full buy‐out possibilities: when main space + Antler Room combined, capacity is reported “as many as 800 guests” in total.
Peak Hours / Visit Times:
Evening hours (around 6:00 pm onward) are busy for after‑work/social groups, especially mid‑week.

Pre‑game/post‑game windows: Because The Loose Moose is located near major venues, many patrons arrive before entertainment or sports events, so 4:00–7:00 pm see elevated traffic.

Late night on weekends (Friday & Saturday nights) up to closing (2:00 am) is also high volume, especially with live music and bar crowd.
Visual Style / Branding Aesthetic:
Rustic industrial with exposed brick, wood beams, communal tables, and high bar stools. Canadian inspired: moose motifs, vintage signage, playful Canadian accents. Bold exterior signage with moose silhouette; warm lighting and approachable vibe.


Energetic, social, and crowd-friendly. Authentic yet playful, unpretentious, emphasizing fun over luxury. Heritage-focused: “Est. 1989,” with taglines like “Part Legend. Part Landmark.”


Moose logo + establishment date for branding and merch. Wood/metal textures, warm lighting, and imagery of people enjoying food & drinks. Earthy, approachable colour palette; informal, welcoming atmosphere.

Name: The Pint Public House
Location: 277 Front St W, Toronto, ON M5V 3G6
Brand Positioning: For downtown Toronto adults looking for a lively, inclusive spot, The Pint Public House delivers a high‑energy, two‑floor sports‑pub experience with great food, drinks, games and event space. Unlike more traditional pubs, it combines big venue excitement with pub comfort hospitality.
Direct or Indirect Competitor:
Direct: Both target the same primary audience: downtown Toronto adults looking for sports viewing, casual drinks, and social/group outings.

Both offer pub-style food, drinks, and multiple screens for sports, and both run promotions and events to attract similar crowds.

Their locations are in overlapping downtown areas and they compete for the same game day, after work, and group outing occasions.

Difference in scale or vibe (The Pint has games/arcade, larger space; Scotland Yard has more traditional pub/football focus) doesn’t move them to indirect as they are still competing for the same occasions and customers, just with slightly different experiences.
Concept Identity:
Large scale, high energy pub and sports bar venue in downtown Toronto that blends “sports‑bar fun + social hang‑out + event space” under one roof.

Location leveraged: “Situated beside the city’s top attractions … five‑minute walk from Union Station and public transportation.”
Target Audience:
Young professionals and groups of friends (ages 21‑35) looking for a lively night out in downtown Toronto.

Sports fans and event‑goers, especially given the venue’s proximity to major attractions like the Rogers Centre and the CN Tower. 

Tourists and visitors looking for an accessible, high‑energy pub experience in the city’s entertainment district. 

Social groups and large gatherings (birthday parties, corporate socials, bachelor/bachelorette events) the venue is set up to accommodate bigger groups and special events.
Atmosphere & Experience: The Pint offers a social, lively, inclusive pub experience downtown Toronto, part sports bar (with big screens and teams), part entertainment venue (games & fun), part casual gathering spot (food + drinks + friends).

The atmosphere is often described as lively, perfect for a social outing, sports-watching, or group get-togethers.

Big fan-fare around the wing specials and generous portion sizes.

Name: Duke of Cornwall
Brand Positioning: A proudly British pub group where guests are welcomed with cold pints, warm hospitality, lively conversation, and homemade comfort food that keeps the tradition alive.
